feedback.text: Hello, I'd like to express my support for the DFA's attempt at curbing of various nefarious industry practices that exploit their userbase for profit with reckless abandon. This goes especially for any and all gambling and gambling-adjacent mechanics in video games that are sold to children or otherwise have a children friendly age rating assigned by a similarly uncaring rating organisation. Game publishers must stop pretending like pushing this sort of things on kids with their parents' debit or credit card is in any way, shape, or form acceptable. I would also like to see the scope of DFA slightly expanded to cover the issue of preserving video games already sold to consumers. As is currently the case, many game publishers prefer to simply kill off their games that they have sold to customers at full price by ending support and shutting down the servers, without which the games become effectively bricked. Physical copies are not a safeguard against this practice, as its the games reliance on those publisher-side servers (and no alternative) that makes this practice possible. One can think of this as an equivalent of a company coming over to your house and breaking your music CDs or movie DVDs, because they decided to no longer support that product. Sounds ridiculous, and yet has been happening to video games for years. The preservation issue is the topic of the Stop Destroying Videogames European Citizens' Initiative, which has garnered over 1.4 million signatures. Contrary to a commonly repeated narrative, it does not require the game publishers nor developers to support their games endlessly. It merely asks for the functionality of the games to be preserved, so that customers who bought those products can play them for as long as they like. How this gets accomplished is up to the developers and publishers, and options are aplenty. Please consider adding protections against this common industry practice to DFA, so that an end of life plan is mandated for any video games sold. While this may not necessarily be the easiest or most economical thing to add to existing games, the new ones that are built from scratch can be easily designed with this in mind. The altered approach to creating such new games may carry slight cost (in the grand scheme of things) associated with redesigning some established approaches, but this is most likely a single time expense, similar to implementing required tools to install seatbelts in cars. Nothing the industry cant handle. Thank you for your time reading this.
